passages.text: The fables of Witchcraft have taken so fast hold and deepe root in the heart of man, that fewe or none can (nowadaies) with patience indure the hand and correction of God. For if any adversitie, greefe, sicknesse, losse of children, corne, cattell, or libertie happen unto them; by & by they exclaime uppon witches.

I thought good (hauing occasion to write upon this subiect) to resolue the doubting reader, and to confute and detect the errors which are in common opinions touching this matter; shewing what is possible and what is impossible, what is natural and what is supernatural.

The greatest barrier to truth in this matter is the fear of those in authority to contradict established opinion, lest they be thought to defend witches. Yet reason and charity demand that we examine what is truly known and what is mere superstition.

chapters.title: Of the Punishment of Innocent People Accused

passages.text: One sort of such as are said to be witches, are women which be commonly old, lame, bleare-eied, pale, fowle, and full of wrinkles; poore, sullen, superstitious, and papists; or such as knowe no religion: in whose drowsie minds the divell hath gotten a fine seat.

These miserable wretches are so odious unto all their neighbors, and so feared, as few dare offend them, or denie them anie thing they aske: whereby they take upon them; yea, and sometimes thinke, that they can doo such things as are beyond the abilitie of humane nature.

These go from house to house, and from doore to doore for a pot full of milke, yest, drinke, pottage, or some such releefe; without the which they could hardlie live: neither obtaining for their service and paines, nor by their art, nothing at all.

It falleth out many times, that neither their necessities, nor their expectation is answered or served, in those places where they beg or borrowe; but rather their lewdnesse is by their neighbors reproved. And further, in tract of time the witch waxeth odious and tedious to her neighbors.

chapters.title: Of Natural Causes Mistaken for Witchcraft

passages.text: A great number of those things which are imputed to witches, happen by reason of naturall causes; as diseases of the bodie, the distemperature of the braine, the fantasticall illusions of the mind, and the disordered humor of the melancholie person.

For as there be diseases which affect the outward parts of the bodie, so are there maladies which trouble the inner faculties of the mind — the imagination being the most prone to strange and terrible delusions, especially in the melancholie.

The physician who understands the natural causes of things shall never be at a loss to explain what others attribute to malevolence. What seems supernatural most commonly has its origin in the body's own disorders.

chapters.title: Of the Absurdity of Common Witchcraft Beliefs

passages.text: It is a common saying, that once a witch always a witch; but how this should be possible, I cannot understand. For if witchcraft be no sinne (as some hold), then why should it be a permanent condition? And if it be the greatest sinne, why is it not treated as such in all courts of law?

Consider the absurdities which must be accepted by those who believe in the powers commonly attributed to witches: that old women of no worldly standing command the forces which shook the foundations of heaven itself — this is beyond all rational belief.

I appeal therefore to the learned, the reasonable, and the charitable: let us not destroy the lives of the innocent through superstition. Let us apply to these matters the same reason and evidence we would demand in any other question of life and death.
